key_insight: Liquid transport growth uses a molten flux and a temperature gradient to decouple dissolution from crystal formation, enabling high-yield single-crystal growth for quantum materials with narrow growth windows or temperature-sensitive stoichiometry.
engineering_relevance: The method offers a practical route to improve crystal quality, yield, and compositional control for research-scale synthesis of quantum materials used in electronics and condensed-matter studies.
method: Use a molten flux as the transport medium instead of a vapor.
Maintain a deliberate temperature gradient to drive continuous solute transport.
Separate dissolution and precipitation spatially so growth can start before full charge dissolution.
evidence_signal: The abstract cites multiple representative materials and practical growth variables, indicating the review synthesizes case studies and design considerations rather than presenting a single new benchmark dataset.
watch_item: LTG still depends on careful control of furnace conditions, melt stability, and ampoule geometry, so reproducibility can be limited by setup-specific optimization.
paper_title: Recent progress on liquid transport growth of quantum materials
